verses.text: Amnon is sick with desire for his half-sister Tamar, and his crafty friend Jonadab teaches him how to trap her. Lust dressed up as love takes counsel with cunning. A wicked friend who helps us get what we sinfully crave is no friend at all, but a snare.

Amnon is sick with desire for his half-sister Tamar, and his crafty friend Jonadab teaches him how to trap her. Lust dressed up as love takes counsel with cunning. A wicked friend who helps us get what we sinfully crave is no friend at all, but a snare.

Amnon is sick with desire for his half-sister Tamar, and his crafty friend Jonadab teaches him how to trap her. Lust dressed up as love takes counsel with cunning. A wicked friend who helps us get what we sinfully crave is no friend at all, but a snare.

Amnon is sick with desire for his half-sister Tamar, and his crafty friend Jonadab teaches him how to trap her. Lust dressed up as love takes counsel with cunning. A wicked friend who helps us get what we sinfully crave is no friend at all, but a snare.

Feigning illness, Amnon has Tamar sent to cook and serve him, then orders everyone else out. Step by step the trap closes, each move seeming innocent. Premeditated sin lays its plans patiently; evil often wears the mask of weakness to disarm its victim.

Feigning illness, Amnon has Tamar sent to cook and serve him, then orders everyone else out. Step by step the trap closes, each move seeming innocent. Premeditated sin lays its plans patiently; evil often wears the mask of weakness to disarm its victim.

Feigning illness, Amnon has Tamar sent to cook and serve him, then orders everyone else out. Step by step the trap closes, each move seeming innocent. Premeditated sin lays its plans patiently; evil often wears the mask of weakness to disarm its victim.

Tamar pleads — do not force me, do no such folly in Israel — but Amnon, stronger, rapes her. She begs even for a lawful way out; he will hear none of it. Lust, once it rules, is deaf to reason, to pity, and to God; it tramples every plea to seize what it wants.

Tamar pleads — do not force me, do no such folly in Israel — but Amnon, stronger, rapes her. She begs even for a lawful way out; he will hear none of it. Lust, once it rules, is deaf to reason, to pity, and to God; it tramples every plea to seize what it wants.

At once Amnon's desire turns to violent hatred, greater than his lust had been, and he has her thrown out and the door bolted. The 'love' that would not be denied despises its object the moment it is satisfied. Sinful passion never loves; having used, it loathes.

At once Amnon's desire turns to violent hatred, greater than his lust had been, and he has her thrown out and the door bolted. The 'love' that would not be denied despises its object the moment it is satisfied. Sinful passion never loves; having used, it loathes.

At once Amnon's desire turns to violent hatred, greater than his lust had been, and he has her thrown out and the door bolted. The 'love' that would not be denied despises its object the moment it is satisfied. Sinful passion never loves; having used, it loathes.

Tamar tears her royal robe, puts ashes on her head, and goes away crying aloud. Her grief is open and bitter, the ruin of an innocent life. Scripture does not hide the suffering of the wronged; it lets us see and feel the real cost of another's sin.

Tamar tears her royal robe, puts ashes on her head, and goes away crying aloud. Her grief is open and bitter, the ruin of an innocent life. Scripture does not hide the suffering of the wronged; it lets us see and feel the real cost of another's sin.

Absalom takes his sister in and tells her to stay quiet; David hears and is angry but does nothing. A father's failure to act lets the wound fester. Absalom says nothing to Amnon — but hatred kept silent is not peace; it is a fire banked, waiting to break out.

Absalom takes his sister in and tells her to stay quiet; David hears and is angry but does nothing. A father's failure to act lets the wound fester. Absalom says nothing to Amnon — but hatred kept silent is not peace; it is a fire banked, waiting to break out.

Absalom takes his sister in and tells her to stay quiet; David hears and is angry but does nothing. A father's failure to act lets the wound fester. Absalom says nothing to Amnon — but hatred kept silent is not peace; it is a fire banked, waiting to break out.

Two years later, Absalom throws a sheepshearing feast and presses David to let all the king's sons come, Amnon among them. Patient, smiling, he sets his trap. Revenge that waits in silence is the most dangerous of all; long-nursed hatred plans coldly and strikes when least feared.

Two years later, Absalom throws a sheepshearing feast and presses David to let all the king's sons come, Amnon among them. Patient, smiling, he sets his trap. Revenge that waits in silence is the most dangerous of all; long-nursed hatred plans coldly and strikes when least feared.

Two years later, Absalom throws a sheepshearing feast and presses David to let all the king's sons come, Amnon among them. Patient, smiling, he sets his trap. Revenge that waits in silence is the most dangerous of all; long-nursed hatred plans coldly and strikes when least feared.

Two years later, Absalom throws a sheepshearing feast and presses David to let all the king's sons come, Amnon among them. Patient, smiling, he sets his trap. Revenge that waits in silence is the most dangerous of all; long-nursed hatred plans coldly and strikes when least feared.

At Absalom's signal his servants kill Amnon at the feast, and the other sons flee in terror. The sword has entered David's own house, exactly as Nathan foretold. The father's secret violence is answered by open violence among his sons; sin sown returns in a bitter harvest.

At Absalom's signal his servants kill Amnon at the feast, and the other sons flee in terror. The sword has entered David's own house, exactly as Nathan foretold. The father's secret violence is answered by open violence among his sons; sin sown returns in a bitter harvest.

A false report races to David that all his sons are dead, and he tears his clothes in grief — until Jonadab, who knew, corrects it: only Amnon is slain. Rumour magnifies sorrow; the truth, though terrible, is less than the terror first feared. Yet the grief is real enough.

A false report races to David that all his sons are dead, and he tears his clothes in grief — until Jonadab, who knew, corrects it: only Amnon is slain. Rumour magnifies sorrow; the truth, though terrible, is less than the terror first feared. Yet the grief is real enough.

Absalom flees, and the surviving sons arrive weeping, and the king and all his servants weep bitterly. The house is drowned in tears. The home that should have been a haven has become a place of crime and mourning — the long, slow ruin sin works in a family.

Absalom flees, and the surviving sons arrive weeping, and the king and all his servants weep bitterly. The house is drowned in tears. The home that should have been a haven has become a place of crime and mourning — the long, slow ruin sin works in a family.

Absalom flees, and the surviving sons arrive weeping, and the king and all his servants weep bitterly. The house is drowned in tears. The home that should have been a haven has become a place of crime and mourning — the long, slow ruin sin works in a family.

Absalom escapes to his grandfather in Geshur and stays three years, while David mourns Amnon and then begins to long for the exiled Absalom. A father's heart is torn between justice and love. David grieves the son he lost and aches for the son who fled, mending neither.
